topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the sterile finality of a judge’s gavel. It was a notification from the university admissions office, referencing a supplemental essay I’d poured months into crafting. Curiosity, initially, had been a faint hum, but as I clicked the attachment, the hum sharpened into a distinct, cold thrum in my chest. It was my narrative, my carefully curated exploration of resilience, now sitting there, polished and presented as a template for future applicants. I reread the paragraphs, the words that felt so intrinsically *mine*, now detached, floating in someone else’s context. My fingers tightened around my mouse until my knuckles were white. A slow, burning heat began to rise in my neck. I closed the laptop with a decisive snap, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet apartment. I stood up, pacing the small living room, the air suddenly feeling too thin, too sharp.

[story 2]

I found the link tucked away in a forgotten folder labeled ‘Drafts.’ The title read, ‘Sample: Overcoming Adversity.’ My breath hitched. It was my essay. Every sentence, every carefully chosen metaphor, was laid bare, sitting beside a stack of other hopeful, equally earnest submissions. A wave of intense discomfort washed over me, starting in my stomach and spreading outward. I stared at the screen, my jaw clenching so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I wanted to slam the keyboard, to shatter the glass screen. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a slow, ragged exhale. My hands began to tremble uncontrollably, and I pressed them against my temples, trying to physically contain the sudden, unwelcome surge of feeling that made my vision swim slightly.

[story 3]

When the notification pinged, I almost didn't look. I was deep into a late-night coding session, the blue light reflecting off my tired eyes. But the subject line—'Your Submission Featured in Our Portfolio'—snapped me to attention. My heart gave a sudden, unpleasant lurch. I clicked. There it was. My voice, my perspective, framed perfectly for prospective students. A strange, hollow sensation settled over me, like a heavy cloak being draped over my shoulders. I sat frozen, staring at the words. A low, involuntary sound escaped my throat—a choked whisper that sounded more like a gasp of disbelief. I slowly pushed my chair back, the scrape of the wheels on the floor sounding deafeningly loud. I walked to the window, pulling the curtain aside, needing the harsh, unfiltered light to try and burn away the feeling that something fundamental had been misplaced.
